theme: Structured thinking — MECE and first principles
objective: Build a correctly structured MECE tree for a real product problem, place 6 provided causes into the correct branches without overlap, identify the first branch to investigate with justification, and challenge one provided assumption using first-principles reasoning.

realWorldAnchor.example: When Zepto evaluated expanding from metro dark stores into Tier 2 cities, the decision required first-principles reasoning — not 'Blinkit is doing it so we should too' but a ground-up decomposition of whether unit economics, demand density, and supply chain assumptions that held in Mumbai would hold in Nagpur.

teamContext: Structured thinking is most visible in the PM's interactions with Engineering leads during problem scoping — when a PM can decompose a problem into mutually exclusive branches, engineering scope becomes cleaner, trade-offs become explicit, and design priorities become obvious to the whole team.

tasks.title: MECE: The Framework That Makes You Think Like a Consultant — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the MECE principle (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) as a thinking tool specifically for product managers — how to decompose problems into branches that have no overlap and no gaps. For you, who has consulting exposure, MECE will feel structurally familiar, but this article adds the product-specific application: breaking down a metric drop, a user complaint spike, or a prioritisation trade-off.
keyTakeaways: MECE means every branch of your breakdown is non-overlapping (mutually exclusive) AND together the branches cover every possibility without exception (collectively exhaustive) — violating either rule produces a flawed analysis that will mislead the team

In PM work, MECE trees are most useful in three situations: diagnosing a metric drop (what could have caused this?), segmenting a user base for feature prioritisation, and structuring a product strategy recommendation to leadership

The hardest discipline in MECE is avoiding the intuitive jump to solutions — your tree branches must describe the problem space completely before you touch solutions, otherwise you will solve the wrong branch

tasks.title: First Principles Thinking — Farnam Street
tasks.summary: Farnam Street's definitive explainer on first principles reasoning — the method of breaking a problem down to its most fundamental truths and building back up from there, rather than reasoning by analogy from what others have done. This is the thinking method behind Jio's free data launch, Zepto's 10-minute delivery model, and CRED's credit-score-only user filter — each was a first-principles bet made by questioning an assumption everyone else accepted.
keyTakeaways: Reasoning by analogy ('Swiggy did X so we should do X') is fast but caps innovation — first principles forces the question: is this assumption actually true, or have we inherited it from someone who inherited it from someone else?

A practical first-principles test for PMs has three questions: (1) Is this actually true? (2) Is it always true regardless of context? (3) Does it have to be true, or is it only true given current constraints that might change?

Jio's 2016 decision to give away free data at scale is the most cited Indian business example of first principles — Mukesh Ambani questioned the assumption that data must be priced as a premium good, concluded it was false given Jio's cost structure, and restructured Indian telecom permanently

tasks.keyword: MECE tree metric drop product manager worked example diagnosis walkthrough PM interview
tasks.whyItMatters: Reading about MECE is the first step; seeing a PM use MECE to walk through a specific metric drop — with the full tree drawn out, branches named, and a hypothesis selected — is where the concept becomes usable. Look for blog walkthroughs or YouTube videos that show the tree visually, not just explain it abstractly.
whatToLearn: What the top-level branches of a metric drop MECE tree typically look like — the most common structures are: internal vs external causes; supply-side vs demand-side; technical vs behavioural; or a funnel-stage breakdown (acquisition / activation / retention / revenue)

How a PM selects which branch to investigate first — the answer combines three factors: which is most likely given available context, which is fastest to verify with existing data, and which has the biggest business impact if the hypothesis is confirmed

tasks.promptTitle: Build a MECE tree for Zomato's Monday order drop
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a PM coach helping me practice MECE structured thinking for product problem diagnosis.

Today I studied:
- The MECE principle: Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive — used to decompose problems without gaps or overlaps
- First principles reasoning: breaking assumptions down to fundamental truths rather than reasoning by analogy
- How Zepto used first principles to evaluate Tier 2 expansion, and how Jio used it to restructure Indian telecom pricing assumptions

Real world context: Zomato's Monday morning order volume dropped 22% compared to last Monday. The CEO wants an explanation by end of day. No additional data has been provided yet.

Your role today: Act as my skeptical PM mentor. When I build my MECE tree, challenge me on every branch — is this truly mutually exclusive from the other branches? Does the tree cover everything plausible? Have I missed an entire category? Push me to think one level deeper, not just validate my first answer.

Activity — 3 rounds:
Round 1: I state my top-level branches (2-4 categories) and explain why they are MECE.
Round 2: For the branch I identify as most likely, I expand it into sub-branches.
Round 3: I pick the most investigable sub-branch and name 3 specific data checks I would run.

After all 3 rounds, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — Monday order patterns in India are shaped by specific local factors: weekly prepaid recharge cycles, salary credit dates, weather patterns affecting delivery areas, and Monday-after-a-holiday effects — a non-India PM might not know to ask about these]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for you]

Begin with: 'Before you build the tree — tell me in one sentence: what question is your MECE tree trying to answer? Then give me your top-level branches.'

bonusTasks.title: Jio first-principles deep dive
bonusTasks.keyword: Jio 2016 free data launch Mukesh Ambani first principles reasoning telecom disruption India product strategy
bonusTasks.summary: Jio's entry into Indian telecom is one of the most documented first-principles product decisions in Indian business history — a company that questioned the fundamental pricing assumption of an entire industry and was proven right at a scale that reshaped half a billion people's internet access. This search gives you a deeply documented real example of what first principles looks like when applied at the highest level. Look for analysis on inc42.com, YourStory, The Ken, or long-form economic newspaper pieces.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Build a complete 2-level MECE tree for Zomato's 22% Monday order drop, place all 6 provided causes into the correct branches, identify the first branch to investigate with justification, and use first-principles reasoning to challenge one of the 6 provided assumptions.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Zomato's Monday order volume dropped 22% compared to last Monday. Here are 6 possible causes flagged by different team members:

1. The payment gateway had 400ms higher latency on Monday morning (flagged by engineering)
2. It rained heavily in Delhi and Mumbai on Sunday night (flagged by operations)
3. Zomato ran a 40% discount campaign last Monday but not this Monday (flagged by marketing)
4. Three popular restaurant chains in Mumbai went offline simultaneously (flagged by restaurant partnerships)
5. A competitor (Swiggy) launched a 'Free Delivery Monday' promo this week (flagged by growth team)
6. The iOS app had a crash on the restaurant search screen affecting users on app version 14.2 (flagged by engineering)

Your deliverables:
A. Build a MECE tree with at least 2 levels. Top-level branches must cover all 6 causes above without any overlap. Place each of the 6 causes under the correct branch — no cause should appear in two branches.
B. Identify which top-level branch you would investigate first. Justify in 3 sentences using: likelihood given available context, speed of verification, and business impact if true.
C. Using first-principles reasoning, identify ONE assumption in the list above that you would question before accepting as fact. State what you would verify and why the assumption might be wrong.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part A: A visual tree using indentation or dashes — top-level branches clearly labelled, sub-branches indented, each of the 6 causes placed at the leaf level with its number. Part B: Three sentences of justification (one each for: likelihood, verification speed, business impact). Part C: One assumption named + a 2-sentence explanation of why you would verify it before accepting it.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 2: Structured Thinking — MECE and First Principles.

I studied today:
- MECE principle: Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive — used to decompose product problems without gaps or overlaps
- First principles reasoning: questioning assumptions rather than accepting them — the Jio and Zepto examples
- How to select which MECE branch to investigate first: likelihood, verification speed, business impact

Real world context I should apply:
Zomato's 22% Monday order drop — a realistic PM diagnosis scenario with 6 flagged causes from different teams.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Build a 2-level MECE tree, place 6 causes correctly, justify which branch to investigate first, and challenge one assumption using first principles.

Here is my work:
[Paste your MECE tree, branch justification, and first-principles challenge here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — especially check for MECE violations: any cause appearing in two branches, or any top-level category that is missing]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about how Monday order patterns in India are shaped by specific local factors — weekly prepaid recharge cycles, salary credit dates, post-Sunday weather, regional holidays — that a non-India PM would miss]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Feature Specification — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
